# Stage 1 Research — Drive (`drive`)

**Identity:** Drive is the artifact store for everything that **isn't** markdown — the binary / non-note outputs of the org's local AI agents (rendered PDFs, images, datasets, exports, generated files), organized by the **ticket** that produced them. Notes holds the thinking (markdown = source of truth); Drive holds the deliverables that thinking produced, and must never become a second source of truth. Like every app it exposes two siblings over one shared state — an MCP agent surface and a rich human UI — built API-first.
**Risk class:** Standard.
Every external fact below is cited inline to a real URL that was fetched during research; version-drifting, secondary, or un-fetched facts are deferred to the consolidated **Verify at build time** list rather than asserted. Today is **2026-07-01**.

> Note on method: this research was produced by a per-topic fan-out (one finder per question) followed by a dedicated adversarial verification pass on the load-bearing storage-backend decision. Precision caveats and date corrections from the verification pass are folded into the text and flagged as **Load-bearing correction** blockquotes where they narrow or fix a raw finding. Citations use inline ([title](url)) links; a deduplicated numbered bibliography is in **§ Sources**.

---

## Executive summary

- **Resolved deferred decision (storage backend): use a plain filesystem + a thin `drive` service — do NOT deploy an object store (MinIO/Garage/SeaweedFS/Nextcloud).** Drive already fronts storage with its own API, UI and MCP surface, so no consumer in the suite ever speaks S3; the two features that matter (out-of-band byte handoff and versioning/provenance) must live in Drive's own API/SQLite layer regardless, so an object-store container is pure operational surface with no offsetting dollar savings. This verdict survived two independent adversarial passes ([Product Impossible](https://productimpossible.com/articles/self-hosted-s3-after-minio/)).
- **MinIO is specifically disqualified for a fresh 2026 adoption** — the community repo was put in maintenance mode (Dec 2025) and **formally archived on 25 April 2026**, the admin console was stripped from the Community Edition in 2025, and upstream steers to the proprietary AIStor product (~$96k/yr entry) ([Blocks & Files](https://www.blocksandfiles.com/ai-ml/2025/06/19/minio-users-complain-after-admin-ui-removed-from-community-edition/1610856), [Glukhov](https://www.glukhov.org/data-infrastructure/object-storage/minio-dead/)).
- **Version + provenance model: content-addressable blobs (SHA-256 = immutable identity + free dedup) + an append-only version-history table in SQLite + a mutable "latest" pointer per (ticket, logical artifact),** with `ticket_id` a NOT-NULL provenance key on every version. This mirrors the suite's git-backed, diffable, reversible audit ethos for non-markdown files without regulatory WORM ([CAS/StoneFly](https://stonefly.com/blog/content-addressable-storage-enterprise-guide/), [AWS S3 delete-marker soft-delete](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/DeletingObjectVersions.html)).
- **Do NOT enable compliance-grade Object Lock / legal hold** on a Standard homelab store — it is irreversible even by root and a foot-gun; the SHA-256 hash plus append-only rows plus audit logging already give tamper-evidence at the right rigor ([AWS Object Lock](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-lock.html)).
- **Byte transfer is out-of-band, always.** MCP carries binaries only as base64-in-JSON and breaks around ~10–11K base64 chars (~8 KB) in real clients while burning ~1 token/char, so the MCP put/get/list tools must exchange metadata + a short-lived fetch/upload reference — never stream artifact bytes — and the browser / pdf app fetch bytes directly over HTTP ([claude-code #54137](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/54137), [Gelembjuk](https://gelembjuk.com/blog/post/file-handling-in-ai-agents-with-mcp-lessons-learned/)).
- **Preview stays a boundary: pdf-renders / drive-stores-and-serves.** Images preview natively, PDFs preview through the sibling `pdf` app's pdf.js viewer pointed at a Range-capable Drive URL, office/doc types are rendered→PDF/PNG by asking `pdf` and the result is cached as a derived artifact; everything else is download-only. Drive never embeds a renderer ([MDN Range requests](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/Range_requests)).
- **The download endpoint is where the real work is:** HTTP Range/206 (or PDF/video seeking degrades to full downloads), a strong ETag = content hash with `If-None-Match`→304 / `If-Range`, correct `Content-Type` from server-side sniffed metadata, `Content-Disposition: attachment` by default (inline only for a strict safe allowlist), and `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` on every response ([MDN Content-Disposition](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Disposition), [MDN nosniff](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/X-Content-Type-Options)).
- **Never serve agent-uploaded SVG or HTML inline** — `image/svg+xml` executes embedded JavaScript (multiple 2025–2026 CVEs/advisories); force attachment + nosniff + a locked asset CSP (`default-src 'none'`), or rasterize SVG server-side and discard the original ([CVE-2026-5026](https://www.sentinelone.com/vulnerability-database/cve-2026-5026/), [SVG security](https://www.svggenie.com/blog/svg-security-best-practices)).
- **MIME is server-verified, not client-trusted:** sniff true type from magic bytes over the leading bytes, validate against a broad allow-list of agent-produced formats (415 on mismatch), persist the sniffed type as canonical; single streaming PUT below ~100 MB, resumable/chunked (tus 1.0 or S3 multipart) above it; whole-object SHA-256 dedup only (block-level CDC is overkill for immutable outputs) ([Transloadit magic numbers](https://transloadit.com/devtips/secure-api-file-uploads-with-magic-numbers/), [tus protocol](https://tus.io/protocols/resumable-upload)).

---

## 1. Storage backend — object store vs plain filesystem (the deferred decision)

**Findings.** ARCHITECTURE.md §9 sets the per-app default as "SQLite for metadata + a file/object store; no shared DB unless research shows a forced dependency" and explicitly defers the concrete backend to research. The reflexive self-hosted choice — MinIO — is in 2026 an active liability rather than a safe default. MinIO relicensed from Apache-2.0 to **AGPLv3** in 2021 ([MinIO official blog](https://www.min.io/blog/from-open-source-to-free-and-open-source-minio-is-now-fully-licensed-under-gnu-agplv3)); the admin/management web console was removed from the Community Edition in 2025, leaving only a basic read-only object browser with bucket/user/policy management pushed to the `mc` CLI or the paid product ([Blocks & Files](https://www.blocksandfiles.com/ai-ml/2025/06/19/minio-users-complain-after-admin-ui-removed-from-community-edition/1610856), [minio Discussion #21316](https://github.com/minio/minio/discussions/21316)); the console now lives only in the commercial **AIStor** Enterprise Edition, which starts around **$96,000/year** and triggered broad community-trust backlash ("not a feature issue, a trust one") ([Futuriom](https://www.futuriom.com/articles/news/minio-faces-fallout-for-stripping-features-from-web-gui/2025/06), [minio Discussion #21326](https://github.com/minio/minio/discussions/21326)); and the community repo went to maintenance mode and was archived, with a community fork (`pgsty/minio`) reverting the console removal to rebuild images ([Vonng](https://blog.vonng.com/en/db/minio-resurrect/), [Glukhov](https://www.glukhov.org/data-infrastructure/object-storage/minio-dead/), [Pepitedata](https://pepitedata.com/minio-community-edition-end-agpl/)).

The live S3-compatible alternatives each have gaps for this use. **Garage** is an AGPLv3 single-binary S3 store with presigned URLs and full multipart, but **explicitly does not support object versioning** (a stub returns "versioning not enabled") and does not implement Object Lock/retention/legal hold ([Garage S3 compatibility](https://garagehq.deuxfleurs.fr/documentation/reference-manual/s3-compatibility/)). **SeaweedFS** is Apache-2.0 (latest 4.37, 2026-06-29) and does implement versioning + Object Lock, but is engineered for billions of small files — overkill for a homelab artifact store — and has an open correctness defect where COMPLIANCE-mode WORM did not actually block deletes as of v4.12 ([seaweedfs GitHub](https://github.com/seaweedfs/seaweedfs), [Issue #8350](https://github.com/seaweedfs/seaweedfs/issues/8350)). **Versity Gateway** (`versitygw`, Apache-2.0, v1.4.1 released 2026-04-22) is the standout for one reason: it is a stateless S3 façade that maps an object key **directly to the identical relative POSIX file path with no proprietary encoding**, so it can be bolted in front of a plain directory tree with zero data migration ([versitygw](https://github.com/versity/versitygw/), [Versity POSIX mapping](https://www.versity.com/versity-s3-gateway-posix-backend/)). **Nextcloud** is the wrong product class entirely — a PHP-FPM monolith needing ~2–4 GB RAM plus Redis/DB that keeps all metadata in its own database and reduces any object store to opaque content-by-id, introducing a second metadata authority ([OpenCloud vs Nextcloud](https://wgall.com/blog/opencloud-vs-nextcloud.html), [Nextcloud primary storage](https://docs.nextcloud.com/server/stable/admin_manual/configuration_files/primary_storage.html)).

Crucially, the S3 API's headline value is largely internal-and-duplicative here: no component in the suite consumes S3 (the `pdf` app previews via Drive's own API, agents via MCP, humans via UI), and the two genuinely valuable S3 features — an out-of-band byte handoff and versioning+provenance — Drive must own at its own API/metadata layer anyway (versioning is a SQLite version chain keyed by ticket; the byte handoff is forced by MCP's base64 limit regardless of backend — see §4/§5). An always-on storage container is therefore pure operational surface for a single operator.

> **Load-bearing correction — MinIO archival date.** The raw finding said "archived as of February 2026." The verified timeline is more precise: MinIO entered **maintenance mode ~3 Dec 2025**, the README was clarified (read as EOL) **~12 Feb 2026**, and the repo was **definitively archived on 25 April 2026** (after a brief un-archive). The server code remains AGPLv3 and the license grant is irrevocable; only maintenance and official binary distribution ended ([Storm Developments](https://stormdevelopments.ca/blog/minio-s-community-edition-is-archived-what-still-runs-in-2026/), [It's FOSS](https://itsfoss.com/news/minio-moves-away-from-open-source/)).

> **Load-bearing correction — don't lead with "AGPLv3" as the disqualifier.** For Drive's actual deployment (MinIO would run as a **separate, unmodified container** talked to over the S3 HTTP API, with no external users receiving a distribution), AGPLv3's network-copyleft is largely a non-issue — MinIO's own `COMPLIANCE.md` says the determination is fact-specific and punts to counsel ([MinIO COMPLIANCE.md](https://github.com/minio/minio/blob/master/COMPLIANCE.md)). Leading with AGPL is also **internally inconsistent**, because Garage — floated as a swap target — is *also* AGPLv3 ([Garage](https://garagehq.deuxfleurs.fr/documentation/reference-manual/s3-compatibility/)). The honest disqualifier for MinIO is **project death**, not the license. (Client SDKs stay Apache-2.0, so speaking S3 to any backend never touches copyleft anyway.)

> **Load-bearing correction — the two named swap targets are NOT equal-cost.** Versity Gateway is byte-transparent (object key = same POSIX path), so filesystem→Versity is a near-zero-migration bolt-on. Garage uses a proprietary on-disk format, lacks versioning and Object Lock, and its value is geo-distributed clustering a single homelab does not need — so filesystem→Garage is a full re-upload. Name **Versity as THE designated swap-path**, not a co-equal of Garage.

> **Load-bearing correction — the real 3am failure mode is write-atomicity, and it favors filesystem anyway.** An object-store PUT gives object+metadata atomicity for free; filesystem+SQLite makes the byte write and the metadata row two non-atomic operations, so a crash between them orphans a blob or dangles a row. This is manageable but is **design work, not a footnote**: implement write-temp → `fsync` → insert-row → atomic-rename, an orphan-sweep job, and correct SQLite WAL / single-writer discipline under concurrent agents. Conversely, on the axis a solo operator fears most — an unrestorable backup — filesystem+SQLite **wins decisively**: it is trivially `restic`/`rsync`-backable, human-inspectable, and single-file restorable, whereas an erasure-coded object backend is opaque (you cannot `ls` your way to one recovered file). So even the strongest steelman for an object store reinforces the filesystem verdict.

**Recommendation.** Adopt **plain filesystem + a thin `drive` service now**, behind a **minimal `put/get/list/delete` storage-driver interface** (deliberately *not* an S3-shaped one — see the correction below). Drive owns artifact bytes on a single mounted Docker volume in a content-addressed layout, keeps all metadata in SQLite (ticket id = provenance key, filename, sniffed content-type, size, sha256, version chain, producing agent, audit rows), and hands large binaries off out-of-band over HTTP so they bypass the MCP/JSON layer entirely. Skip MinIO (dead upstream), SeaweedFS (built for billions of small files; buggy WORM), Garage (no versioning; proprietary format), and Nextcloud (wrong product class). **Explicit cheap swap-path if this is ever wrong:** if a genuine external S3 consumer ever materializes, front the *same* filesystem tree with **Versity Gateway** (Apache-2.0, object-key = identical POSIX path, zero data migration because files stay files) — that transparent bolt-on is the actual insurance, and it is precisely why paying for an S3 abstraction *today* is unjustified.

> **Load-bearing correction — trim the two cargo-culted elements the finder carried over from cloud practice.** (1) **Do not S3-shape the internal boundary.** A `put/get/list/delete` driver is enough; an S3-shaped interface is speculative generality (YAGNI) whose swap targets are feature-incomplete versus what SQLite already gives you, and Versity can front the raw POSIX tree even if the abstraction is imperfect. (2) **Signed-URL handoff is contested — treat it as an open decision, not a default.** The MCP base64 limit demands an *out-of-band* handoff, which does not by itself demand *cryptographic signing*: Drive already sits behind the proxy + auth gateway, so a proxy-authenticated download endpoint (stream from disk after the forward-auth check; nginx-style `X-Accel-Redirect`/`sendfile` where the edge supports it — note the suite's edge is Caddy, whose `reverse_proxy`/`file_server` handles Range natively but has no `X-Accel-Redirect` equivalent) avoids a signing key to rotate and expiry clock-skew bugs. App-minted short-TTL HMAC tokens are only strictly required if a byte fetch must work for a client the auth gateway cannot see (e.g. a future external S3 consumer). Carry both to Planning (see Open decisions).

---

## 2. Versioning + provenance (link-back to ticket / note)

**Findings.** S3/MinIO object versioning gives each mutation a unique, opaque, immutable version id; prior versions stay retrievable regardless of later writes, and a DELETE without a version id does not erase data — it inserts a 0-byte **delete marker** that becomes the current version (GET returns 404-like) while every prior version remains retrievable by id; only DELETE with an explicit version id permanently removes ([AWS Versioning](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/Versioning.html), [AWS Deleting object versions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/DeletingObjectVersions.html), [MinIO versioning README](https://github.com/minio/minio/blob/master/docs/bucket/versioning/README.md)). This "soft delete + delete marker" pattern is directly reusable as Drive's tamper-evident delete semantics **without adopting S3**. Object Lock/WORM has GOVERNANCE (privileged bypass), COMPLIANCE (irreversible until expiry, not even root), and legal hold (indefinite) modes, aimed at SEC 17a-4(f)/FINRA-tier regulatory retention — far beyond a Standard app's needs and hazardous because mistakes are permanent ([AWS Object Lock](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-lock.html), [MinIO object locking](https://blog.min.io/object-locking-versioning-and-holds-in-minio/)).

Content-Addressable Storage hashes content (SHA-256 is the common choice) and uses the digest as the permanent address: identical content yields the same hash (free dedup), any change changes the hash (self-verifying, inherently immutable) — the mechanism behind Git, OCI registries, and IPFS ([Abilian CAS](https://lab.abilian.com/Tech/Databases%20&%20Persistence/Content%20Addressable%20Storage%20(CAS)/), [StoneFly](https://stonefly.com/blog/content-addressable-storage-enterprise-guide/)). For provenance that must survive outside the DB, note S3 user metadata (`x-amz-meta-*`) is **immutable** after upload while object **tags are mutable** — a common mix-up; provenance belongs in the immutable channel (or immutable DB rows), tags in mutable operational state ([AWS UsingMetadata](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingMetadata.html), [CloudThat tags vs metadata](https://www.cloudthat.com/resources/blog/understanding-amazon-s3-object-tags-and-metadata-in-aws)).

> **Load-bearing correction — SeaweedFS WORM is not a safety guarantee today.** SeaweedFS advertises versioning + Object Lock, but has open correctness bugs when versioning and locking combine, including COMPLIANCE-mode WORM not enforcing before the retention date (delete still succeeds, creating a delete marker instead of AccessDenied) ([Issue #8350](https://github.com/seaweedfs/seaweedfs/issues/8350), [Issue #7194](https://github.com/seaweedfs/seaweedfs/issues/7194)). Do not rely on it as an immutability guarantee without re-testing.

**Recommendation.** Keep latest + full history via **CAS blobs + append-only version rows**: store each uploaded byte-stream once as a content-addressable blob keyed by SHA-256 (natural dedup, immutable, self-verifying); layer a SQLite schema on top — an `artifacts` row = one logical, ticket-keyed artifact with a mutable `current_version_id` pointer, and an append-only `artifact_versions` table = the immutable history (never UPDATE/DELETE; new upload = new row; deletion = a delete-marker row, exactly like S3). Recommended fields (Stage-2 will finalize): `blobs(content_hash PK, size_bytes, storage_path, refcount, created_at)`; `artifacts(artifact_id, ticket_id NOT NULL, note_id NULL, logical_name, current_version_id, created_by, created_at)`; `artifact_versions(version_id PK, artifact_id FK, content_hash FK, ticket_id NOT NULL [denormalized — versions can span tickets], note_id NULL, producing_agent_id, mime_type, size_bytes, checksum_algo, is_delete_marker, created_at)`, indexed on `(ticket_id)` and `(artifact_id, created_at DESC)` for reverse list-by-ticket and latest-per-artifact. **Immutability stance for a Standard app: content immutability yes, regulatory WORM no** — do not enable COMPLIANCE-mode Object Lock or legal hold; the SHA-256 is the integrity proof, version rows are append-only, and every state change is audit-logged (Stage-5). If an S3 backend is ever introduced, GOVERNANCE-mode is the only lock worth considering, and ticket/note/agent provenance should *also* be stamped as immutable `x-amz-meta-*` so it survives outside the DB — but SQLite stays the rebuildable index, and the CAS hash + files are the durable record (root "files = truth, DB = index" invariant).

---

## 3. Preview via the `pdf` app

**Findings.** The boundary is **`pdf` renders, Drive stores-and-serves** — Drive must not reimplement rendering (per its CLAUDE.md). In-browser PDF viewing (pdf.js, which the `pdf` app almost certainly wraps) depends on **HTTP Range requests** for progressive/page-on-demand loading: the client sends `Range: bytes=…`, a supporting server advertises `Accept-Ranges: bytes` and answers `206 Partial Content` + `Content-Range`, an out-of-bounds range returns `416`, and a server that ignores Range returns the whole `200` body ([MDN Range requests](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/Range_requests)). pdf.js range loading has historically been buggy/disabled on Safari/iOS and can be broken by proxies that reject Range with 403 — so Drive **and** the Caddy edge must pass Range through untouched ([pdf.js #9824](https://github.com/mozilla/pdf.js/issues/9824), [pdf.js #8425](https://github.com/mozilla/pdf.js/issues/8425), [Bugzilla 1022833](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1022833)).

`Content-Disposition: inline` displays in-page; `attachment` forces Save-As with an optional `filename`/RFC-5987 `filename*` (browsers strip path info) ([MDN Content-Disposition](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Disposition)). Forcing attachment neutralizes stored-XSS from active types; the real-world failure is serving user-uploaded SVG/HTML **inline** as `image/svg+xml`, which executes embedded JS — repeatedly exploited (CVE-2026-5026 serving `image/svg+xml` inline; SVG-upload XSS in PrivateBin, Nextcloud, and others) ([CVE-2026-5026](https://www.sentinelone.com/vulnerability-database/cve-2026-5026/), [PrivateBin advisory](https://github.com/PrivateBin/PrivateBin/security/advisories/GHSA-cqcc-mm6x-vmvw), [Nextcloud SVG XSS](https://hackerone.com/reports/894876)). `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` forces the browser to honor the declared Content-Type verbatim (no HTML re-interpretation) — required on every byte response, but only helps if the declared type is **correct**, so MIME must be validated on upload (§4) ([MDN nosniff](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/X-Content-Type-Options)). For any active/untrusted type, defense beyond attachment is an asset-level CSP (`default-src 'none'; script-src 'none'; object-src 'none'`) + sandboxed iframe, or server-side rasterization ([SVG security best practices](https://www.svggenie.com/blog/svg-security-best-practices), [DigiNinja SVG XSS](https://digi.ninja/blog/svg_xss.php)).

The presigned/tokenized-URL model (whether S3 or an app-minted equivalent) lets the browser or `pdf` app fetch bytes directly instead of proxying through Drive's JSON/MCP API, and can set `Content-Disposition`/`Content-Type` **per download** at URL-generation time (S3's signed `response-content-disposition`/`response-content-type` params) — so one stored object serves as inline preview *or* named attachment without duplicating storage; it supports GET (download/preview), HEAD (size probe for the viewer), and PUT (upload) ([AWS presigned URLs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/using-presigned-url.html), [AWS GetObject params](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/API_GetObject.html), [Ben Nadel](https://www.bennadel.com/blog/2696-overriding-content-type-and-content-disposition-headers-in-amazon-s3-pre-signed-urls.htm)).

**Recommendation.** Route preview by type: **images** (png/jpg/webp/gif) serve inline, browser-native; **PDFs** hand off to the `pdf` app's pdf.js viewer pointed at a short-TTL Drive URL that supports Range; **office/doc/other renderables** — Drive calls the `pdf` app to render → PDF/PNG, then **stores the returned preview as a derived artifact** keyed to `(source_artifact, render_version)` and serves it from cache, so `pdf` is never a live dependency of every preview view; **everything else** is download-only. Drive's byte endpoint MUST implement: `Accept-Ranges: bytes` + full Range/206/`Content-Range`/416/200-fallback; a strong **ETag = content hash** with `If-None-Match`→304 and `If-Range`; `Content-Type` from validated stored metadata (never request-sniffed); `Content-Disposition: attachment` by default, `inline` only for a strict allowlist (images, pdf, `text/plain`) and **never** for `text/html`/`image/svg+xml`; `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` on every response; and a locked CSP + sandboxed iframe for any active type ever previewed. Confirm the `pdf` app's input contract (fetch-by-URL vs by-path vs bytes; which formats; page-1 thumbnail support) so the handoff matches without reimplementing rendering.

> **Load-bearing correction — Range must survive the Caddy edge.** Some proxies strip or 403 Range requests, silently degrading pdf.js progressive loading and video seeking to full downloads. Verify Range/`If-Range`/206 pass through Caddy unbuffered end-to-end; do not assume Safari/iOS seeking behaves identically to Chromium.

---

## 4. Content types, size limits, dedup + ticket addressing

**Findings.** MIME must be verified **server-side from magic bytes**, not trusted from client Content-Type or file extension (both trivially spoofed): read the leading bytes, sniff with libmagic/file-type, reject mismatches with **415**, and use an explicit **allow-list**, not a deny-list ([Transloadit](https://transloadit.com/devtips/secure-api-file-uploads-with-magic-numbers/), [PkgPulse](https://www.pkgpulse.com/blog/file-type-vs-mime-types-vs-mmmagic-file-2026)). libmagic is heuristic (byte patterns at fixed offsets over a ~125-class taxonomy) and should be paired with allow-lists, size limits, and deeper scanning for **polyglots** (e.g. an `MZ`/PE or HTML tag appearing far from file start) ([Transloadit](https://transloadit.com/devtips/secure-api-file-uploads-with-magic-numbers/)). `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` makes the browser respect the declared type and enables Cross-Origin Read Blocking for HTML/TXT/JSON/XML, so downloads should combine it with `application/octet-stream` + `attachment` ([MDN nosniff](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/X-Content-Type-Options)).

Size/resumability: S3 multipart uses 5 MiB–5 GiB parts (no minimum on the last), max 10,000 parts, max object 48.8 TiB, and AWS recommends switching from single-PUT to multipart at ~100 MB ([AWS qfacts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/qfacts.html)). The **tus** resumable-upload protocol (v1.0.0) is a small HTTP core — HEAD to discover `Upload-Offset`, PATCH (`application/offset+octet-stream`) to append, POST to create — plus Creation/Checksum/Expiration/Termination/Concatenation extensions, adopted by Cloudflare/Supabase/Vimeo with a Go reference server (`tusd`) ([tus protocol](https://tus.io/protocols/resumable-upload), [tusd](https://github.com/tus/tusd)). Dedup: **whole-object SHA-256 content addressing** gives automatic dedup + integrity ([StoneFly](https://stonefly.com/blog/content-addressable-storage-enterprise-guide/), [Sohil Ladhani CAS](https://sohilladhani.com/blog/post/2026-04-08-content-addressable-storage/)); block-level content-defined chunking (restic's Rabin-fingerprint CDC, ~1 MiB average) is far more machinery than a store of immutable, mostly-unique outputs needs ([restic CDC](https://restic.net/blog/2015-09-12/restic-foundation1-cdc/)). The Caddy edge has **no default body-size limit** — set `request_body max_size` (413 over-limit), scoped per-route, at/just above the app cap ([Caddy request_body](https://caddyserver.com/docs/caddyfile/directives/request_body), [Softcolon](https://www.softcolon.com/blogs/limiting-request-body-file-upload-size-in-caddy/)).

**Recommendation.** On every upload: stream to disk (never buffer whole files in memory), sniff true type from magic bytes, validate against a broad allow-list of agent-produced formats — `application/pdf`, `image/png|jpeg|gif|webp|svg+xml` (treat SVG as active content → force download), `text/csv`, `application/json`, `application/zip`, the OOXML office types, and `application/octet-stream` as catch-all — reject others with 415, and persist the **sniffed** MIME as canonical (keep the client-declared value only as an untrusted hint). Serve every download with the sniffed Content-Type, `nosniff`, and `Content-Disposition: attachment` except the §3 safe-inline allowlist. Set a homelab max object size (~5 GB); single streaming PUT below ~100 MB, resumable above — prefer **tus 1.0** (Checksum + Expiration extensions; `tusd` reference) for resume-across-disconnect on flaky homelab links; set Caddy `request_body max_size` just above the cap on upload routes so the proxy 413s abuse without truncating legitimate chunks. Do **whole-object SHA-256 dedup only**: compute the digest while streaming, store bytes once under `blobs/<sha256>` with a refcount, let many logical artifacts/tickets reference one blob, and GC a blob only when refcount hits zero (dedup composes cleanly with the §2 version chain). **Ticket addressing:** give each artifact a stable `artifact_id` (UUIDv7 for time-ordering; the content SHA-256 is a *separate* dedup key so re-uploads/versions stay distinguishable); logical key layout `ticket/<ticket_id>/<artifact_id>/<filename>` as the SQLite index over physical `blobs/<sha256>`; an agent holding only a `ticket_id` calls an MCP `list_artifacts(ticket_id)` tool to enumerate.

---

## 5. MCP agent surface + human UI over one API

**Findings.** The two surfaces are siblings over one API (root invariant): the MCP agent surface is **put / get / list by ticket**, and the human UI **browses, previews, downloads**. The binding constraint on the MCP side is that MCP resources carry binary data only as base64 blobs, and large-binary transfer over MCP is a known anti-pattern: base64 inflates size, clients often refuse to decode blobs or blow token limits, and a documented Drive-MCP `create_file` bug rejects/silently truncates base64 above ~10–11K chars (~8 KB) ([MCP Resources spec](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/server/resources), [claude-code #54137](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/54137), [MCP limitations](https://medium.com/@ckekula/model-context-protocol-mcp-and-its-limitations-4d3c2561b206)). The spec's guidance is to return small metadata/pages plus a reference (URL/handle), not bytes — for web-fetchable content servers SHOULD use an `https://` scheme only when the client can fetch it directly ([MCP Resources spec](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/server/resources), [MCP discussion #1197](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/discussions/1197)). The **MCP File Uploads Working Group** (charter 2026-04-23, SEP-2356, still Draft) scopes declarative file-input descriptors for client→server uploads, notes server→client delivery is nominally covered by Resources/`BlobResourceContents`, and lists presigned upload URLs / streaming / chunked transfer as candidate approaches — i.e. presigned URLs are the emerging idiom, not yet baked into the protocol ([MCP File Uploads charter](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/community/file-uploads/charter), [working group](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/community/working-groups/file-uploads)).

**Recommendation.** Build API-first: a single HTTP+SQLite core service, with the MCP surface and the UI as thin siblings over it. **MCP tools** (names indicative; Stage-2 finalizes): `put_artifact(ticket_id, filename, content_type_hint, …) → {artifact_id, version_id, upload_ref}` returning a short-lived out-of-band **upload** reference (the agent PUTs bytes there, not through MCP); `get_artifact(artifact_id | ticket_id+name) → {metadata, download_ref}` returning metadata + a short-lived **download** reference; `list_artifacts(ticket_id) → [metadata…]`. Every tool exchanges **metadata + a reference, never base64 bytes**. The **UI** browses by ticket, previews via §3's routing, and downloads via the same references — so both surfaces exercise one shared state (the SQLite index + CAS blobs) with neither downstream of the other. Provenance is enforced in Drive's metadata DB and checked **before** any upload/download reference is minted, so every put/get/preview traces to its originating ticket. Do **not** design Drive assuming a finalized MCP upload spec exists — build the HTTP out-of-band path yourself and treat MCP file-input descriptors (SEP-2356) as a future nicety; re-check the idiomatic way to declare file inputs / return download references against the pinned MCP SDK at build (ARCHITECTURE §9 mandates verifying MCP SDK/transport at build).

---

## 6. Security + audit baseline (Standard) — carry-forward Stage-5 checklist

Drive is **Standard** risk class, so Stage-5 = input validation + authz on every endpoint + authz on the MCP surface (agents are scoped users) + rate/WIP limits + audit logging of state changes + no secrets in logs. The edge (Caddy + `auth` forward-auth) handles TLS, HSTS, and identity-header trust per the proxy/auth research; the items below are Drive-specific and carry forward into Stage-5.

- [ ] **Authz on every endpoint and every MCP tool** — the operator (UI) and each agent (MCP) are scoped `auth` principals; validate the token `aud`=drive and required scope locally, and check ticket-scoped access before any list/get/put or reference mint ([MCP Resources spec](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/server/resources)).
- [ ] **Server-side MIME validation** — sniff magic bytes, allow-list, 415 on mismatch, scan deeper for polyglots; persist the sniffed type as canonical, never the client-declared value ([Transloadit](https://transloadit.com/devtips/secure-api-file-uploads-with-magic-numbers/), [PkgPulse](https://www.pkgpulse.com/blog/file-type-vs-mime-types-vs-mmmagic-file-2026)).
- [ ] **`X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` on every byte response** + `Content-Disposition: attachment` by default; `inline` only for the strict safe allowlist ([MDN nosniff](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/X-Content-Type-Options), [MDN Content-Disposition](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Disposition)).
- [ ] **Never serve untrusted SVG/HTML inline** — force attachment + nosniff + locked asset CSP (`default-src 'none'; script-src 'none'; object-src 'none'`) + sandboxed iframe, or rasterize SVG server-side and discard the original ([CVE-2026-5026](https://www.sentinelone.com/vulnerability-database/cve-2026-5026/), [SVG security](https://www.svggenie.com/blog/svg-security-best-practices)).
- [ ] **Out-of-band byte references are bearer-scoped and short-TTL** — mint only after the ticket-provenance/authz check, scope to a single object + method, keep TTL short; the reference (signed token or proxy-authenticated route — see Open decisions) is the authz boundary once bytes leave the JSON API ([AWS presigned URLs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/using-presigned-url.html), [dev.to presigned expiry](https://dev.to/zhizhiarv/important-notes-on-s3-presigned-url-expiration-3ga9)).
- [ ] **Never stream artifact bytes through the MCP surface** — metadata + reference only; base64-in-JSON breaks ~8–10 KB and burns tokens ([claude-code #54137](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/54137), [Gelembjuk](https://gelembjuk.com/blog/post/file-handling-in-ai-agents-with-mcp-lessons-learned/)).
- [ ] **Body/size limits + resumable-upload abuse controls** — Caddy `request_body max_size` at/just above the app cap per upload route (413 over-limit); enforce the real per-artifact cap in the app; expire abandoned tus uploads ([Caddy request_body](https://caddyserver.com/docs/caddyfile/directives/request_body), [tus Expiration](https://tus.io/protocols/resumable-upload)).
- [ ] **Rate / WIP limits** on put and reference-mint per agent identity (keyed off the `auth` principal), consistent with the suite's per-agent budget model.
- [ ] **Audit logging of all state changes** — every put / new version / delete-marker / reference mint records `(principal, ticket_id, artifact_id, version_id, action, timestamp)` as an append-only trail; no token/secret material in logs.
- [ ] **Write-atomicity + integrity** — write-temp → `fsync` → insert-row → atomic-rename; orphan-sweep job for crash-interrupted writes; verify stored SHA-256 on read where cheap; SQLite WAL / single-writer discipline under concurrent agents.
- [ ] **SQLite is a rebuildable index, not a second source of truth** — the CAS blobs (+ any object metadata) are the durable record; Drive must not canonicalize markdown (root invariant).

---

## Open design decisions for the operator

- **Byte-handoff mechanism:** app-minted short-TTL HMAC/presigned URLs vs a proxy-authenticated out-of-band download endpoint (stream-from-disk after forward-auth)? The two adversarial passes split on this; signing is only strictly required if a byte fetch must serve a client the `auth` gateway cannot see. Decide before Stage-2 fixes the download contract.
- **Derived-preview lifecycle:** should rendered previews (office→PDF/PNG, PDF page-1 thumbnails) be cached as first-class derived artifacts with their own version/GC, and are they themselves ticket-provenance-keyed to the source artifact?
- **Max artifact size + resumable threshold:** confirm the homelab cap (~5 GB?) and the single-PUT→tus cutoff (~100 MB?), and whether tus is worth the dependency vs plain chunked PUT.
- **Content-type allow-list scope:** exactly which agent-produced formats are accepted (415 everything else), and is SVG accepted at all (download-only) or rejected outright?
- **Delete semantics exposed to agents:** does the MCP surface expose delete at all, and is it always a soft delete-marker (never hard delete), with hard delete / blob GC operator-only?
- **Dedup visibility across tickets:** when two tickets produce byte-identical artifacts they share one blob — is that acceptable, or must provenance isolation forbid cross-ticket blob sharing (refcount vs copy)?
- **Note linkage:** is `note_id` on an artifact version mandatory or optional, and does Drive validate it against the Notes app or store it opaquely?
- **Swap-path trigger:** what concrete event (a real external S3 consumer? multi-node?) would justify bolting Versity Gateway in front of the filesystem tree — pre-agree it so the decision isn't re-litigated under pressure.

---

## Verify at build time

- **MinIO / fork state:** whether `pgsty/minio` (or any community fork) is actively maintained at build, and whether upstream `minio/minio` remains archived — fast-moving; re-confirm before citing MinIO's status.
- **Versity Gateway (`versitygw`):** latest release past v1.4.1 and current object-lock/versioning coverage on the POSIX backend, before choosing it as the S3 escape hatch ([versitygw](https://github.com/versity/versitygw/)).
- **SeaweedFS:** latest version and whether the COMPLIANCE-mode WORM enforcement bug (#8350) and versioning+lock conditional-write bugs (#7194 and successors) are fixed, *if* SeaweedFS is ever reconsidered ([Issue #8350](https://github.com/seaweedfs/seaweedfs/issues/8350)).
- **Garage:** latest release and whether object versioning has graduated from stub to supported (the S3-compatibility page is the authoritative check) ([Garage](https://garagehq.deuxfleurs.fr/documentation/reference-manual/s3-compatibility/)).
- **MCP SDK/transport + File Uploads WG:** exact SDK/transport version and status of SEP-2356 / the File Uploads WG at build — confirm the idiomatic way to declare file inputs and return download references, and whether each Drive MCP surface is Streamable-HTTP vs stdio ([MCP File Uploads charter](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/community/file-uploads/charter)).
- **`pdf` app input contract:** does it fetch by URL, by path, or accept bytes; which input formats (docx/office? images?); page-1 thumbnail support; and its viewer's Range requirements — verify against the `pdf` app's own research/build once it exists.
- **Caddy edge behavior:** that Range/`If-Range`/206 pass through unbuffered/untransformed and that `request_body max_size` is set per upload route ([Caddy request_body](https://caddyserver.com/docs/caddyfile/directives/request_body)).
- **pdf.js cross-browser Range:** Safari/iOS progressive-loading behavior in the operator's actual browser matrix (bugs are version-drifting across pdf.js versions) ([pdf.js #9824](https://github.com/mozilla/pdf.js/issues/9824)).
- **Presigned response-header overrides:** if an S3 backend is ever used, confirm it honors signed lowercase `response-content-disposition`/`response-content-type` and SigV4 expiry identically to AWS (some non-AWS implementations ignore them) ([AWS GetObject](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/API_GetObject.html)).
- **CAS digest choice:** whether SHA-256 remains the right dedup/integrity digest vs BLAKE3 for Drive's throughput on local compute — benchmark at Stage-6.
- **Secondary-source facts to re-confirm against primaries:** the MinIO 2026 archival/maintenance timeline and any successor's maturity are drawn partly from blogs (Blocks & Files, It's FOSS, Storm Developments, Vonng, Glukhov, Pepitedata) — re-verify the repo state and pick accordingly.

---

## How the Stage-1 exit criteria are met

**(a) Every deferred decision relevant to Drive has a researched recommendation.**
- **Storage backend (the primary ARCHITECTURE §9 deferral):** resolved — **plain filesystem + thin service, SQLite metadata, out-of-band byte handoff; no object store**, adversarially verified, with Versity Gateway named as the explicit cheap swap-path (§1).
- **Versioning + immutability model:** resolved — CAS blobs + append-only version rows + delete markers; content immutability yes, regulatory WORM no (§2).
- **Provenance / ticket-and-note linkage:** resolved — mandatory NOT-NULL `ticket_id` on artifacts and versions, indexed for list-by-ticket, optional `note_id` (§2, §4).
- **Preview architecture:** resolved — `pdf`-renders / Drive-serves, with derived-preview caching so `pdf` is not a live dependency (§3).
- **Byte-transfer / MCP surface shape:** resolved — metadata + out-of-band reference, never base64 (§5), with the signing-vs-proxy-auth handoff surfaced as an operator decision.
- **Content types, size limits, dedup, addressing:** resolved — server-side magic-byte MIME allow-list, tus/multipart above ~100 MB, whole-object SHA-256 dedup, `ticket/<id>/<artifact_id>/<filename>` addressing (§4).

**(b) All external-system facts are cited, not assumed.** Every substantive claim carries an inline ([title](url)) link to a fetched primary or a flagged secondary source; version-drifting, un-fetched, or secondary facts are quarantined in **Verify at build time** rather than asserted. As a **Standard**-class app, Drive integrates no external live system beyond the sibling `pdf` app (whose exact contract is itself deferred to build) — there is no Wazuh/CMDB-style external verifier to study at this stage, so the "external-system API + exec-safety study" rigor of Critical-infra does not apply.

---
